proof_sketch: In max_reduction_dim2_kernel, warp 0 (which performs the final reduction) executes ~10 additional instructions (conditional read, warp_reduce_max loop, output write) after the shared memory write, while warps 1-7 retire immediately. This instruction-count disparity ensures that by the time warp 0 reads warp_maxs[1..7], all other warps' writes have completed. No value-based variation can alter this timing because the workload is uniform per thread (each thread processes exactly one element) and only warp id determines the extra work. Therefore the race condition never manifests on the tested hardware.

No value-based variation can alter this timing because the workload is uniform per thread (each thread processes exactly one element) and only warp id determines the extra work. Therefore the race condition never manifests on the tested hardware.",
  "survival_reason": "The mutation removes a __syncthreads() after writing to shared memory, creating a data race. However, due to the extra instructions in warp 0 (the final reduction), all other warps finish their shared memory writes long before warp 0 reads those values. The warp scheduler naturally completes the shorter warps first, making the read safe. No matter what numerical values are used, this timing remains unchanged because the instruction count difference is fixed by the code structure. Hence every test with any input yields a bitwise-identical result, and the mutation is effectively equivalent under the fixed-shape contract.",

  "kill_strategy": "No value-level test can force a different result because the race condition is prevented by the inevitable instruction-order disparity between warp 0 and other warps.",
  "suggested_test": null,
  "recommendations": "Even when warp divergence naturally serializes execution, always use explicit __syncthreads() to guarantee correctness across all GPU architectures and driver versions. The apparent equivalence observed here is hardware-specific and not guaranteed by the CUDA memory model."
}
